title: Sternbergia citrina
taxonomy: Sternbergia citrina is classified within the family Amaryllidaceae, which is a large group of flowering plants that also includes daffodils and hyacinths. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, with Sternbergia citrina belonging to the subfamily Scilloideae. The genus Sternbergia itself is a small group of bulbous perennials native to the Mediterranean region and surrounding areas. Sternbergia citrina is a specific species within this genus, known for its bright yellow flowers and early spring bloom. The classification of Sternbergia citrina reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Amaryllidaceae family.

side_effects: Sternbergia citrina is known for its potential skin and eye irritation due to its sap and alkaloids. Handling the plant with bare hands can cause skin irritation, including redness and itching. Ingesting any part of the plant can be toxic and may lead to nausea, vomiting, and other gastrointestinal issues. The sap of Sternbergia citrina may also cause allergic contact dermatitis in some individuals. If ingested or applied to the skin in large quantities, the plant may cause more severe symptoms.
intro: Sternbergia citrina, also known as the yellow Sternbergia, is a perennial bulbous plant native to the Mediterranean region, belonging to the Amaryllidaceae family. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, making it beneficial for treating various health issues such as respiratory problems, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Sternbergia citrina include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. In traditional medicine, Sternbergia citrina is often used to prepare herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are commonly used to treat respiratory and gastrointestinal disorders.
